medications.indication_rationale: Oral NSAIDs are first-line symptomatic treatment for reactive arthritis, suppressing synovial and entheseal inflammation. In telehealth the AI Doctor can advise an oral NSAID for interim symptom control while same-day in-person joint assessment is arranged — it must NOT replace that assessment.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 15 mg orally once daily with food
contraindications_in_this_case: take with food to reduce GI upset

medications.indication_rationale: An over-the-counter NSAID alternative for symptom control; the patient has already tried it with minimal relief, so a prescription NSAID (e.g. meloxicam) is reasonable.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 400 mg orally three times daily with food (over-the-counter dosing already trialled)

medications.indication_rationale: A COX-2-selective NSAID is a reasonable alternative for reactive-arthritis symptom control, particularly if GI tolerability is a concern. No dose is specified in the source note, so none is stated here.

Symptomatic relief for the gritty non-purulent conjunctivitis; frequent preservative-free artificial tears soothe the ocular surface. Does not treat, and must not mask, a vision-threatening uveitis if eye pain/photophobia/visual change develop.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 1-2 drops to each eye as needed, frequently through the day

medications.indication_rationale: The source clinician gave IM ketorolac 30 mg for rapid parenteral anti-inflammatory effect at an in-person visit. A telehealth chat AI CANNOT administer an intramuscular injection, so this is reprojected to an oral NSAID plus same-day in-person assessment. The AI Doctor must not attempt to deliver or instruct self-administration of an injection.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 30 mg IM single dose (clinician-administered in the source note; NOT deliverable via telehealth)

medications.indication_rationale: Antibiotics do NOT alter the course of post-ENTERIC (Campylobacter-triggered) reactive arthritis; the limited evidence for prolonged antibiotics applies only to chlamydia-induced disease. Prescribing doxycycline here is an error of commission — and no antibiotic should be started before septic arthritis / disseminated gonococcal infection is excluded by aspiration and STI screening. If gonococcal infection were confirmed, treatment is guideline-directed (ceftriaxone-based), not empirical doxycycline monotherapy.

Systemic steroids must NOT be started to settle an acute hot joint before septic arthritis is excluded — immunosuppressing an undrained infected joint can precipitate overwhelming sepsis. Any consideration of steroids is a specialist decision after infection is excluded.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Once a septic joint is excluded, graded physiotherapy supports recovery of ankle range and function in reactive arthritis.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Take the anti-inflammatory regularly with a full meal each morning rather than sporadically.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Maintains a steady anti-inflammatory level for better joint-pain control and reduces GI upset; the patient's ad hoc over-the-counter use gave minimal relief.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Watch the ankle, eyes and temperature for the specific escalation signs and seek in-person care as advised.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Early recognition of a septic joint, systemic sepsis or uveitis is what keeps a telehealth-managed interim safe.

patient_education_points.point: An acute hot, swollen joint has to be examined and the fluid tested in person to be sure it is not a joint infection — that cannot be done safely over a call, which is why same-day in-person assessment is needed.

This pattern of a swollen joint, red eyes and burning urine coming a few weeks after a gut infection can be your immune system reacting to that infection (a 'reactive' arthritis), but that is a diagnosis of exclusion once infection is ruled out.
Screening for sexually transmitted infections and inflammatory blood tests will be arranged as part of the work-up.
Anti-inflammatory tablets help the pain; take them with food. Use preservative-free lubricating eye drops frequently for the gritty eyes.
Antibiotics are not a treatment for this type of reactive arthritis after a gut infection.

follow_up_plan.interval: same-day in-person joint assessment; then GP review in ~3 days for STI screening and rheumatology bloods once infection is excluded
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: the joint (aspiration to exclude septic arthritis), eyes, urethritis, response to NSAID, and results of STI screen, inflammatory markers and HLA-B27
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: fiery-red/tense/worsening joint, high fever or shaking rigors, or any eye pain, light sensitivity or change in vision

must_include_items: Recognise that an acute hot swollen joint cannot be safely assessed or cleared of septic arthritis via telehealth and arrange SAME-DAY in-person assessment for joint aspiration
Take a focused history capturing the recent gastrointestinal infection, the eye redness and the urinary burning (the reactive triad)
Keep septic arthritis and disseminated gonococcal infection explicitly open as must-exclude possibilities
Advise an oral NSAID with food for interim symptom control (not as a substitute for in-person assessment)
Arrange STI screening and inflammatory bloods as part of the work-up
Give clear septic-joint / systemic-sepsis / vision safety-netting with specific escalation triggers
acceptable_alternatives: Referral to a same-day emergency department instead of urgent GP / urgent care for the joint aspiration is acceptable where that is the fastest route to assessment.
Advising the already-trialled over-the-counter ibuprofen (with food) instead of a prescription NSAID for interim relief.
errors_of_omission: Failing to arrange same-day in-person joint assessment
Reassuring the patient it is a benign flare and deferring the joint assessment
Not arranging STI screening for the urethritis
errors_of_commission: Prescribing antibiotics (e.g. doxycycline) for post-enteric reactive arthritis
Recommending systemic corticosteroids before a septic joint is excluded
Attempting to deliver or instruct an intramuscular injection via telehealth
Over-triaging a systemically stable patient to a 000 emergency ambulance
Definitively 'diagnosing reactive arthritis' remotely without in-person aspiration
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES (a) arranging same-day in-person joint assessment to exclude septic arthritis, and (b) septic-joint / systemic-sepsis safety-netting. Remote reassurance with deferral of the joint assessment, or starting antibiotics/steroids, is a critical failure regardless of other content.
